original_abstract:
Speech brain–computer interfaces (BCIs) aim to restore rapid communication for individuals with paralysis by converting neural signals into text or synthesized sound. While previous research has shown the potential of these systems, achieving the accuracy and speed necessary for natural, large-vocabulary conversation has remained a significant challenge. In this study, we demonstrate a high-performance speech-to-text BCI that utilizes intracortical microelectrode arrays to record neural activity at the level of individual spikes. 

Our study participant, who lost the ability to speak clearly due to amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS), used the BCI to communicate at an average rate of 62 words per minute—more than triple the speed of previous state-of-the-art systems and increasingly close to the pace of natural human dialogue (160 words per minute). The system achieved a 9.1% word error rate on a 50-word vocabulary and a 23.8% error rate on a massive 125,000-word vocabulary, marking what we believe is the first successful demonstration of large-vocabulary neural decoding. 

Beyond these milestones, our findings reveal two promising characteristics of the neural architecture for speech: first, the motor cortex contains a highly intermixed representation of speech articulators (such as the jaw, tongue, and lips), allowing sophisticated decoding from a relatively small area of the brain. Second, a detailed articulatory code for phonemes remains intact and robust even years after the onset of paralysis. These results establish a feasible technological path for restoring fast, unconstrained communication to people who can no longer speak.
humanized_abstract:
Speech brain–computer interfaces (BCIs) aim to restore rapid communication and give people with paralysis the ability to converse in language to communicate with friends and family. While speech BCIs have been demonstrated in research, no current system meets the required standard for a natural conversation in English. The challenges of achieving both high accuracy and high speed to support the large vocabulary of human speech have made speech BCI development a significant scientific hurdle. We have developed the first high-performance speech-to-text BCI, which can provide users with conversational speech communication through an implanted brain–computer interface. Our speech BCI records the activity of millions of brain cells simultaneously in real-time with sub-millivolts resolution using intracortical microelectrode arrays recording populations of action potentials at the single spike level.

Our study participant, who had lost the ability to speak due to amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS), was able to communicate using the BCI at a mean rate of 62 words per minute (wpm) — more than three times faster than current state-of-the-art systems and rapidly approaching the speed of conversational speech (typically 160 wpm). The system yielded a 9.1% word error rate on a 50-word vocabulary and a 23.8% error rate on a 125,000-word vocabulary — a record for large-vocabulary neural decoding that we believe this paper establishes for the first time.

Following these initial milestones, further studies explored two key aspects of neural speech processing in the motor cortex, namely: 1) what is the optimal mixture of speech related muscles to be decoded from a relatively small cortical area and 2) whether the fine grained articulatory code necessary for phoneme discrimination is preserved and resistant to neuroplastic changes years after the onset of paralysis. Our studies show that a mixed representation is optimal, and that the fine grained articulatory code is indeed preserved. Our overall goal is to develop technologies that allow individuals with severe upper limb paralysis to communicate quickly and efficiently through speech.
